sources.notes: For every prefix, maximize the last position before a strict gcd drop over all reorderings of that prefix.
Official editorial maintains counts of divisors and answers with the largest divisor-count that is not equal to the current prefix length.
Used to confirm the divisor-count criterion and the Fenwick optimization over frequencies of counts.

annotation.summary: For each prefix, output the largest possible index where the prefix gcd can strictly decrease after reordering that prefix.
annotation.constraints: Total n and values are up to 2e5, so divisor lists and incremental counts are feasible.
annotation.core_idea: A strict gcd drop after k elements means there exists a divisor d that divides the first k arranged elements but not the next one. For a prefix of length i, if d divides c_d elements and c_d<i, we can place those c_d elements first and get a drop at k=c_d. Thus the answer is max c_d over all divisors with c_d<i. Maintain divisor occurrence counts as the prefix grows, and maintain which counts currently appear using a Fenwick tree or an active candidate list.
annotation.complexity: O(N log N + total_divisors log N) after divisor precomputation.
tricks: exclude divisors common to all prefix elements

solution_variants.name: divisor-count-fenwick
solution_variants.summary: Precompute divisors, update each divisor's frequency when a new value arrives, and query the largest frequency below the prefix length.
solution_variants.complexity: O(N log N + total_divisors log N)

solution_variants.name: active-divisor-list
solution_variants.summary: Track only divisors whose current count may still become the answer and update the maximum by candidate lists.
solution_variants.complexity: O(N sqrt[3] N) style bound from the official implementation notes

tags.evidence: The condition for a gcd drop is characterized by a divisor d that divides exactly c_d prefix elements.
The algorithm groups prefix elements by their divisors and optimizes over divisor frequencies.
The Luogu implementation maintains how many divisor counts have each frequency with a Fenwick tree.
